facts_disclosed: Dating Soph — 8 months, met at climate rally

turns.content: ok so i need advice on something. my girlfriend wants to come to lunar new year dinner at my parents house and im like... excited but also nervous?? for reasons
That sounds like a big step! What's making you nervous about it? Is it about your family's reaction to her, or more about the general pressure of bringing a partner to a family event?

ok so context. my parents know about soph and they're fine with it. like when i came out at 17 they didnt make a big deal of it which in asian parent language means they accept it but theyre not gonna like throw a pride parade about it you know. they just quietly started setting an extra place at the table whenever i mentioned i was seeing someone and thats honestly the most my parents way of showing love

That's actually really lovely in its own way — the quiet acceptance through actions rather than big declarations. Setting an extra place at the table is such a meaningful gesture, even if it's understated.

So if they already know about Soph and they're accepting, what's the nervous part? Is it more about how Soph will handle the family environment, or something else?

yeah exactly its more about soph fitting in. like lunar new year at my parents place is heaps full on... my whole extended family is there and my aunties are gonna ask questions and its loud and theres a million dishes and everyone speaks a mix of cantonese and english and soph is very much a white australian girl from the suburbs lol. shes super open minded obviously but its different being IN it vs just hearing about it

That's a totally valid concern. It's one thing for Soph to be supportive and interested in your culture from the outside, and another to be dropped into a big family gathering where she doesn't speak the language and doesn't know the customs.

A few thoughts:

1. **Prep her beforehand.** Walk her through what to expect — the food, the noise level, the aunties asking personal questions, the language switching. If she knows it's coming, it won't be overwhelming.

2. **Give her a role.** People feel less awkward when they have something to do. Maybe she can help carry dishes, or you can teach her one Cantonese phrase to use (your family will love it).

3. **Be her anchor.** Stay near her, translate when needed, include her in conversations. Don't disappear into the kitchen for 30 minutes and leave her alone with the aunties.

How long have you two been together?

about 8 months now. we met at a climate rally last year which is genuinely the most melbourne thing that has ever happened to anyone lol. she was holding a sign about fossil fuel divestment and i was like... oh no shes hot AND politically engaged. it was over for me

Ha! Meeting at a climate rally IS peak Melbourne. That's an amazing meet-cute story though — politically engaged and attractive is a powerful combination.

Eight months is a solid amount of time. You're past the early "everything is perfect" phase and into actually knowing each other. Has she met your parents at all yet, or would this be the first time?

shes met my mum and dad a couple of times when shes come over for dinner but those were chill nights just us. lunar new year is like a completely different vibe. my auntie helen alone is going to interrogate her about what she does and whether she can cook and probably try to feed her until she explodes

Ha, the auntie interrogation is unavoidable — that's just how it goes. But honestly? If Soph has already met your parents a couple of times and those went well, that's a really good sign. She's not walking in totally cold.

For Auntie Helen and the others:
- **Warn Soph about the food thing.** In a lot of Chinese families, pushing food is love. Tell her to just accept it graciously and eat what she can. She doesn't have to finish everything.
- **The "what do you do" questions are coming.** Prep Soph with a simple answer. What does she study or do?
- **Let your parents be the bridge.** Since they've already met her, they can introduce her to the extended family in a way that signals "she's welcome here."

What does Soph think about it? Is she nervous too, or more excited?

shes genuinely excited which is sweet but also makes me more nervous lol. she does arts slash philosophy at uni so i already know auntie helen is gonna be like 'but what JOB will you get' and i'll have to do damage control. soph thinks its funny when i tell her about my family but living it is different

The arts/philosophy thing is DEFINITELY going to get the "but what's the career plan" treatment from the aunties. That's just universal Asian family energy. But honestly, if Soph already finds your family stories funny rather than off-putting, she's got the right attitude.

Here's a strategy for the career question: coach Soph to pivot quickly. Instead of defending philosophy, she can say something like "I'm really interested in [specific practical-sounding thing]" — even if it's vague. Aunties just want to hear something they can nod at.

Also — and this is important — your nervousness is coming from love. You love your family and you love Soph and you want both worlds to mesh. That's a good problem to have. The fact that your parents are already welcoming tells you a lot about how this will go.

ok when you put it like that its less scary. i think part of it too is that lunar new year is like... its my favourite family thing. i love the chaos of it and the food and my nai nai always gives the best red envelopes and everyone is happy. i dont want to be stressed about soph the whole time instead of just enjoying it

That makes total sense — you don't want to spend the whole night in host/translator/buffer mode when you should be enjoying your favourite family tradition. That's a really reasonable concern.

But here's the thing: the prep work you do NOW is what lets you relax on the night. If you:

- Brief Soph on what to expect (the noise, the food pushing, the questions, the language mix)
- Introduce her to a couple of key family members early so she has people she's "met"
- Accept that some awkward moments will happen and they'll be funny later

...then you can actually step back and enjoy it. You won't need to hover because she'll know what's coming.

Also, eight months in, I'd trust that Soph can handle herself. She's clearly someone who shows up to things and engages — I mean, she was at a climate rally. She's not going to be a wallflower at dinner.
lol thats a really good point actually. the girl will literally chain herself to a government building for climate justice she can probably survive my auntie helen asking if she can make dumplings

Exactly! If she can face down fossil fuel companies she can handle dumpling interrogation. And honestly, if she says she WANTS to learn to make dumplings, Auntie Helen will probably adopt her on the spot. That's the cheat code right there.

I think this is going to go better than you expect. Your family sounds warm even if they're intense, Soph sounds like she genuinely wants to be there, and you've already laid the groundwork with those earlier dinners.

the dumpling cheat code... i am writing that down lol. ok i feel heaps better about this. im gonna talk to soph this week and go through the whole rundown of what to expect and who everyone is. maybe ill make her a little family cheat sheet with names and how theyre related to me

A family cheat sheet is genuinely genius. Names, relationships, key facts ("Auntie Helen — will ask about your career and try to overfeed you. Uncle Brendan — talks about fishing for 45 minutes. Nod along.") That kind of thing. Soph will appreciate the effort and it shows you're taking this seriously.

Has Soph ever been to a big Chinese family dinner before, or is this completely new territory for her?

nope first time. she grew up in like a very small nuclear family situation... christmas with four people kind of thing. so this is going to be a culture shock for sure but in a good way i think. she keeps saying she wants to understand my world better which is lowkey the sweetest thing anyone has ever said to me

That is really sweet — and it tells you something important about her. She's not just tolerating your family and culture, she actively wants to be part of it. That kind of genuine curiosity and effort goes a long way.

And honestly, going from Christmas-with-four-people to lunar new year chaos might be a shock, but most people love the energy once they're in it. The warmth, the food, the noise — it's infectious. She'll probably come out of it wanting to come back next year.

What Cantonese phrase are you going to teach her? "Gong hei fat choy" is the obvious one but if she drops something more unexpected your relatives will lose their minds.

oh my god imagine if she said "sihk jo faahn mei ah" to my nai nai... nai nai would actually die of happiness. ok that might be too advanced but gong hei fat choy is definitely happening at minimum. soph is a quick learner though she already knows how to say thank you from coming to dinner at ours

The fact that she already picked up "thank you" from dinner visits — that's a great sign. She's paying attention and making effort without being asked. Your nai nai is going to adore her.

Start with gong hei fat choy and see how she goes. If she's keen, add one more phrase. But don't overwhelm her — one confident phrase is better than three mumbled ones.

You've got a great plan. Brief her, make the cheat sheet, teach her the phrase, and then just enjoy your lunar new year.

honestly thank you this helped so much. i was spiralling about it and now i actually have a plan instead of just vibes of anxiety. ok im off to go plan this cheat sheet this is genuinely going to be so fun once i stop overthinking it

That's the spirit! Stop overthinking, start cheat-sheeting. Lunar new year with your family AND your girlfriend there — that's going to be really special. Have fun with the planning, and happy new year in advance!
